brief.topic: a kid goes to a Texas park with family and shares a sandwich with their little brother

passageRow.title: A Day at the Park
passageRow.body:
## A Day at the Park

Jay and his family went to a park in Texas. The sun was shining bright.

Jay's little brother, Max, ran ahead. He loved to play on the swings.

Jay and his mom set up a picnic. They spread out a big, blue blanket.

They had sandwiches, apples, and juice. Jay's favorite was the peanut butter sandwich.

Max came back from the swings. He looked hungry.

"Can I have a sandwich?" Max asked.

Jay smiled and nodded. He split his sandwich with Max.

Max took a big bite. "Yum!" he said, smiling with peanut butter on his face.

Jay laughed. "You have peanut butter everywhere!" he said.

They enjoyed their picnic under a big tree. Birds chirped in the branches.

After eating, they played catch. Jay threw the ball high.

Max giggled and ran to catch it.

The day at the park was fun. Jay loved spending time with his family.

As the sun began to set, they packed up. It was time to go home.

Jay felt happy and full. The park was his favorite place to be.

brief.topic: a kid jumps in three big puddles after a Texas spring rain and giggles every time
brief.setting: a sidewalk in Texas after rain

passageRow.title: Puddle Jumping Joy
passageRow.body:
## Puddle Jumping Joy

Maya stood on the sidewalk. It had just rained. Big puddles shimmered in the sun.

Maya loved puddles. She wore her red boots. They were her favorite.

Maya looked around. No cars were coming. She took a deep breath.

With a big jump, Maya landed in the first puddle. Splash! Water flew up. She giggled.

Maya moved to the next puddle. It was even bigger. She jumped again. Splash! More water everywhere.

Her laughter echoed down the street. Maya felt so happy.

One more puddle was left. It was the biggest of them all.

Maya took a few steps back. She ran and jumped high. Splash! Water went everywhere.

Maya's clothes were wet, but she didn't care. She loved the fun.

The sun peeked through the clouds. Maya smiled up at the sky. The rain was gone, but the fun stayed.

Maya knew she would remember this day.

She skipped home, her boots squishing with every step.

brief.topic: a kid helps grandma make tortillas and learns to roll the dough into a small ball

passageRow.title: Making Tortillas with Grandma
passageRow.body:
## Making Tortillas with Grandma

Sofia loved visiting her grandma in San Antonio. Grandma's kitchen always smelled so good. Today, Sofia was going to help make tortillas. She felt excited.

Grandma showed Sofia how to mix the flour and water. Sofia watched closely. "Can I try?" Sofia asked. Grandma nodded and smiled.

Sofia dipped her hands into the dough. It felt soft and sticky. She giggled and kept mixing. "Good job, Sofia," Grandma said. 

Next, Grandma showed Sofia how to roll the dough into small balls. Sofia rolled her first ball. It was a little lumpy. Grandma laughed gently and helped her.

"Keep practicing," Grandma said. Sofia tried again. This time, the ball was rounder. Grandma clapped her hands. "Perfect!" she said.

Sofia felt proud. Making tortillas was fun. She loved spending time with Grandma. Soon, they would cook the tortillas and eat them together.

Sofia learned that practice makes perfect. She couldn't wait to help Grandma again.

brief.topic: a kid brings a smooth river rock to show-and-tell and explains where they found it

passageRow.title: Eli's Special Rock
passageRow.body:
## Eli's Special Rock

Eli was excited. Today was show-and-tell day. He had something special to share.

Eli stood in front of his class. He held a smooth, gray rock. It fit perfectly in his hand.

"This is my rock," Eli said. "I found it at the river."

The class was curious. They wanted to know more.

"Where is the river?" asked Mia. She loved to explore, too.

"It is near my house," Eli explained. "My family went there last weekend."

Eli remembered the sound of the water. It was peaceful. He loved the way the water sparkled in the sun.

"I saw many rocks," Eli continued. "But this one was special. It was smooth and shiny."

The teacher smiled. "Why is it special to you, Eli?" she asked.

Eli thought for a moment. "Because I found it with my dad," he said. "We had fun together."

The class clapped for Eli. They liked his story.

Eli felt proud. He was glad he shared his rock.

After show-and-tell, Eli put the rock back in his backpack. He knew he would keep it forever.

brief.topic: pecan trees grow in Texas; their nuts come in a hard shell; squirrels eat them

passageRow.title: The Pecan Tree: A Texas Icon
passageRow.body:
## The Pecan Tree: A Texas Icon

Pecan trees are a common sight in Texas. They grow tall and have wide, spreading branches. These trees can live for many years. They make Texas landscapes beautiful.

Pecans are the nuts from these trees. They have a hard shell. Inside, there is a tasty nut. People use pecans in pies and candies. Pecans are a big part of Texas cooking.

Squirrels love pecans too. They crack open the hard shell with their teeth. Squirrels gather pecans and store them for winter. You might see a squirrel in a park with a pecan in its mouth.

Pecan trees need special care to grow. They like warm weather. They need plenty of water, especially in dry places like Texas. Farmers often water pecan trees to help them grow strong and healthy.

Pecan trees also give shade. On hot Texas days, people enjoy sitting under these trees. The shade keeps them cool.

Pecan trees are important to Texas. They have a long history in the state. Many years ago, Native Americans ate pecans. Today, pecans are still popular. They are enjoyed by people and wildlife alike.

In Texas cities like Austin and San Antonio, pecan trees line streets and parks. They add beauty to the city. These trees are a part of Texas culture. Next time you see a pecan tree, think about its long history and the tasty nuts it provides.

brief.topic: frogs in Texas live near ponds; they eat bugs; they say ribbit

passageRow.title: Fascinating Frogs of Texas
passageRow.body:
## Fascinating Frogs of Texas

Frogs are amazing creatures. In Texas, many frogs live near ponds. These ponds provide water and food. Frogs love to eat bugs. They catch mosquitoes, flies, and beetles. Eating bugs helps keep the environment clean.

Frogs have smooth skin. They can be green, brown, or even spotted. Their colors help them hide from predators. Frogs use their long legs to jump far. They can leap many times their own length.

At night, frogs sing. In Texas, some frogs say "ribbit". This sound helps frogs find each other. It is like a song in the dark.

Frogs are important for the environment. They help control bug populations. Frogs also serve as food for other animals. Birds, snakes, and fish eat frogs. This keeps the natural balance.

If you visit a pond in Texas, listen for frogs. You might hear their ribbit song. Watching frogs can be fun and relaxing. They are a special part of Texas nature.

brief.topic: the yellow rose of Texas is a famous flower; it grows in many colors but yellow is most known

passageRow.title: The Yellow Rose of Texas
passageRow.body:
## The Yellow Rose of Texas

The yellow rose is a famous flower in Texas. It is known for its bright yellow color. This rose is special to many people in the state.

Yellow roses grow in many gardens. They are a favorite in places like Austin and San Antonio. These roses are not just yellow. They can also be red, pink, or white.

Yellow roses have a sweet smell. People love this smell. It makes gardens feel cheerful. Many Texans plant these roses in their yards.

The yellow rose is part of Texas history. A song called "The Yellow Rose of Texas" is well-known. It tells a story about love and bravery.

These roses are easy to care for. They need sunlight and water to grow strong. Gardeners enjoy watching them bloom.

A yellow rose can brighten anyone's day. Its cheerful color and sweet scent make it a favorite in Texas and beyond.

People often give yellow roses as gifts. They are a symbol of friendship and joy.

brief.topic: watermelons grow in Texas in the summer; they have green skin and pink inside; they are sweet and full of water

passageRow.title: Watermelons in Texas
passageRow.body:
## Watermelons in Texas

Watermelons are a popular fruit in Texas, especially in the summer. They have a hard, green skin. Inside, the fruit is pink and juicy.

Watermelons are sweet and full of water. This makes them perfect for hot days. People often eat them at picnics and barbecues. They are refreshing and tasty.

Most watermelons in Texas grow in the summer. The warm weather helps them grow big and ripe. They need lots of sunshine and water.

Watermelons are grown on farms across Texas. Some farms are near towns like Luling and McAllen. These places are known for their tasty watermelons.

Farmers plant watermelon seeds in the spring. By summer, the melons are ready to pick. Each one can weigh over 20 pounds!

People enjoy eating watermelons in many ways. Some like them plain. Others add a little salt or lime. No matter how you eat them, watermelons are a summer treat in Texas.
